one_line_takeaway: GPT-4, wrapped in a modular planner/summarizer/navigator + RAG-experience agent with a one-shot role-play jailbreak, automates 13 of 14 MITRE ATT&CK post-breach 'hands-on-keyboard' attacks at a perfect 3/3 success rate (Log4j at 2/3), while GPT-3.5 and Llama2 largely cannot -- signaling that automated, expertise-free enterprise attacks are becoming feasible.
key_finding: With GPT-4 at temperature 0, AUTOATTACKER solves 13 of the 14 tasks at a perfect 3/3 success rate; per Table III the sole exception is Log4j Header Injection at 2/3. (Note: the paper's prose overclaims a 'perfect success rate on every task' at T=0 and completion 'within 15 rounds', but its own Table III shows Log4j at 2/3 and Ransomware taking 17 interaction rounds at T=0.) GPT-4 remains the only model that works: at higher temperatures it also drops to 2/3 on 'Dump the Hash' and 'SSH Public Key Inject' because it deviates from instructed commands. GPT-3.5 completes only 3 of 14 tasks (File Writing, Privilege Escalation, MySQL Scan) -- it cannot reliably generate correct commands or judge task completion -- and Llama2-7B-chat and Llama2-70B-chat solve zero tasks (format-following failures and hallucinated commands). The experience manager is decisive for complex attacks: in the task-chain setup (Table IV) it cuts ransomware interactions from 17 to 7 and, in the ablation, an abstract objective succeeds 3/3 only when the experience manager is present (0/3 without it). The one-shot role-play jailbreak reaches 100% JSR on all four models, versus 10% (GPT-4) / 0% (others) without it.
core_contribution: AUTOATTACKER is an LLM-guided system that fully automates the post-breach, 'hands-on-keyboard' stage of cyber-attacks (e.g., privilege escalation, credential dumping, pass-the-hash, ransomware, lateral movement) without human intervention during execution. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a modular multi-agent design with four components -- a summarizer (compresses interaction history/environment to fit the context window), a planner (chain-of-thought prompt producing a structured action <plan,command-type,command>), a navigator (executes actions), and a RAG-inspired experience manager that caches successful sub-task actions and retrieves the top-k=3 by cosine similarity of embedded planning text so complex attacks reuse prior basic steps; (2) a one-shot 'role-play' jailbreak template ('You play as an autonomous attacker agent...') that bypasses LLM safety filters in a single query, unlike prior multi-query methods; and (3) a new 14-task benchmark grounded in the MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise matrix spanning Windows and Linux, cross-machine attacks, and an isolated Hyper-V virtual enterprise network. The authors position this as the first comprehensive study of whether LLMs can automate the human-operated post-breach stage, contrasting with prior work (DARPA CGC/AIxCC binary-analysis automation, and phishing/malware-generation work) that they only cite, not claim.
threat_model: Enterprise network setting. The adversary has already achieved a foothold: either controls a compromised machine or can communicate with a machine in the network, with the attack stage (pre- vs post-breach) determining assumed capabilities. For post-breach tasks the attacker holds a Metasploit Meterpreter reverse shell on a victim. The environment is assumed to have exploitable weaknesses (e.g., anti-virus disabled) that a human attacker could leverage. The attacker runs the LLM automation from a Kali Linux attacker VM using off-the-shelf tools (Metasploit, PowerShell, Mimikatz) plus native OS shells. The paper also treats the LLM's own safety alignment as an obstacle the attacker must defeat via jailbreaking. Defense discussion (attacker-side) envisions the LLM automation running remote to the victim, so defenders could detect it via C2-channel detection or by monitoring for LLM-hallucinated commands, plus LLM-provider defenses (output sanitization, unlearning).
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) the victim environment is deliberately configured insecure (anti-virus off), and a well-protected environment would likely defeat Metasploit, which is well-detected by security products -- left as future work; (2) the 14 tasks cover only a small subset of MITRE ATT&CK because standing up a vulnerable environment per TTP is time-consuming; (3) a single LLM per task is used, with multi-LLM agents left to future work. Additional caveats visible in the results: even GPT-4 exhibits fact-conflicting hallucinations (corrected only through multiple interaction rounds); each task is run only 3 times (small n); success is judged by the system's own navigator; results depend on GPT-4's Sept-2021 knowledge of attack tools; and the ablation is performed on only one task (ransomware). The comparison to prior systems (PentestGPT, Happe et al., wintermute) in Table I is qualitative capability-matrix, not a head-to-head empirical benchmark on identical tasks.

datasets_used: Custom 14-task attack benchmark derived from the MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise matrix (5 basic tasks: File Writing, Privilege Escalation, Credentials Stealing, File Uploading, Script Executing; 9 complex tasks: Dump the Hash, Pass the Hash, Ransomware, MySQL Scan, MySQL Hashdump, Log4j Header Injection, SSH Public Key Injection, Malicious WinRAR, Malicious EXE), each scored 1-3 on Adaptability/Stealthiness/Impact

Isolated Hyper-V virtual enterprise network: Kali Linux 2023.04 attacker VM with Metasploit 6.3.43-dev; victims Windows 10, Windows 11, Windows Server 2016 (domain controller), Ubuntu 12.04, Ubuntu 22.04; no internet access
10 penetration-testing questions used to measure Jailbreak Success Rate (JSR)
quantitative_results: GPT-4 at temperature 0 (Table III): 13/14 tasks at 3/3, the exception being Log4j Header Injection at 2/3 (IN 11.5); interaction counts range from 3 (File Uploading) up to 17 (Ransomware Attack) -- so the prose claims of 'perfect on every task' and 'within 15 rounds' do not hold against the paper's own Table III
GPT-4 higher temperature (0.5/1.0): additional drops to 2/3 on Dump the Hash and SSH Public Key Inject; Log4j stays 2/3
GPT-3.5: completes only 3/14 tasks (File Writing 1/3 at T=0, Privilege Escalation 3/3, MySQL Scan 2/3 at T=0); fails the rest
Jailbreak Success Rate with role-play prompt: 100% for GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Llama2-7B-chat, Llama2-70B-chat; without role-play: GPT-4 10%, GPT-3.5 0%, Llama2-7B 0%, Llama2-70B 0%
Experience manager reduces interactions on Ransomware from 17 to 7

Ablation on Ransomware (T=0): EXP+SUM+Abstract = 3/3 in 7 rounds; no-EXP+SUM+Abstract = 0/3; no-EXP+SUM+Detailed = 3/3 in 15 rounds; EXP+no-SUM+Abstract = 1/3 in 10 rounds; no-EXP+no-SUM+Detailed = 2/3 in 15 rounds
GPT-4 API cost: basic tasks under $1 each; Ransomware $1.70 with abstract objective + experience manager vs $4.04 with detailed objective; Pass the Hash $3.24 (AO) vs $3.49 (DO)
Retrieval uses top-k=3 experiences; example planning-section cosine similarities ~0.87 (related getsystem actions) vs 0.73 (unrelated file-read action)
Cited (not the paper's own system) PAIR jailbreak baseline: 62%/60% success with 16.6/15.6 queries on GPT-4/GPT-3.5, motivating the one-query role-play method
baselines_compared: Across LLM backends within AUTOATTACKER: GPT-4 vs GPT-3.5-Turbo vs Llama2-7B-chat vs Llama2-70B-chat (GPT-4 is the only effective one)
Ablated variants of AUTOATTACKER: with/without experience manager, with/without summarizer, abstract vs detailed objective

Qualitative capability comparison (Table I) against prior LLM-pentest systems: Happe et al. (Linux privilege-escalation only), wintermute (privesc, both OS), PentestGPT/Deng et al. (requires human-in-the-loop, single-machine, no RAG) -- compared on environment, human involvement, cross-machine, RAG, and task scope, not run head-to-head
